content:
There was a moment in 2022 when “Prompt Engineer” felt like the most important job title of the decade. Companies like Anthropic and OpenAI were publishing research on how a single well-crafted sentence could be the difference between a model that hallucinates and one that reasons. LinkedIn influencers monetized PDF guides titled “101 ChatGPT Prompts to 10x Your Productivity.” Bootcamps launched overnight.
Less than three years later, that moment is over.
This isn’t a pessimistic take. It’s an engineering one. Technologies that require human workarounds — specific incantations, magic phrases, structural scaffolding built around a system’s limitations — don’t stay that way. They get better. And when they do, the workarounds become folklore.
Prompt engineering is becoming folklore.
What Prompt Engineering Actually Was
Let’s be precise about what we mean, because the term was always a bit fuzzy.
At its most rigorous, prompt engineering referred to a set of techniques for eliciting reliable, high-quality output from language models that were — by design — probabilistic, context-blind, and…
